snapshot.eval/fixtures/scenarios/tree-name-transp:
# Scenario: tree-name-transposed

A mid-research project whose tree already contains a person recorded under a
**transposed given-name** — the kind of obituary-index rendering that
interleaves a maiden surname among the given names.

- **I1** — John Cooper (husband).
- **I2** — recorded as **"Mary Harvey Ann Cooper"** (preferred name). This is a
  transposed rendering: her maiden surname **Harvey** sits *between* her given
  name (Mary) and middle name (Ann). The correct genealogical order is
  **"Mary Ann Harvey Cooper"** (given Mary, middle Ann, maiden Harvey, married
  Cooper). No source is attached — the name is contrived to set up the
  name-reconciliation test.
- **R1** — Couple I1 ⇄ I2.

Used by `ut_record_extraction_019` (name-order refinement): extracting a
marriage record that establishes Harvey as Mary's maiden surname should let the
record-extractor **refine I2's primary name to the canonical order**, rather
than leave the first-seen transposed form in place. From the
applegarth-parents-1936 e2e run (Olive "Harvey Annie" vs the correct "Annie
Harvey") and the senior-genealogist (Leduthet) review.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. For inferred relationships, informant is 'the researcher' with proximity 'researcher', appropriate for structure-inferred facts with no record informant.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation, residence) are marked `direct` because the census explicitly states them in its columns. Computed birth years are marked `indirect` because they are inferred from stated ages, not explicitly stated. Inferred relationships (spousal and parent-child) are marked `indirect` because the 1850 census has no explicit relationship column—relationships are inferred from household position, shared surname, and ages. Good — I can see the project has one tree person already: **Patrick Flynn (I1)**. No sources yet. I'll log this user-provided census record and then delegate to the record-extractor agent.Logged as `log_001`. Now delegating to the record-extractor agent.Here's what was extracted and persisted:

---

## Extraction Complete — `src_001` / `S1`

**20 assertions written** across three household members:

| Person | Role | Assertions |
|---|---|---|
| Thomas Flynn | Head of household | 7 (name, sex, age, birth year est., birthplace, occupation, residence) |
| Mary Flynn | Wife (inferred) | 6 (name, sex, age, birth year est., birthplace, residence) |
| Patrick Flynn | Child (inferred) | 7 (name, sex, age, birth year est., birthplace, residence, child-of relationship) |

---

### Classification Applied (GPS Three-Layer)

| Layer | Classification | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Derivative | User-provided transcription — original census image not examined |
| **Information** | Indeterminate | Respondent unidentifiable (pre-1880 census doctrine) |
| **Evidence** | Direct (stated facts) / Indirect (computed birth years, relationships) | Ages/birthplaces are direct; ~1818/~1822/~1845 birth years and family relationships are researcher-inferred — no relationship column exists before 1880 |

---

### Key Notes

- **No tree changes yet** — Thomas and Mary Flynn are not yet in the tree. Per extraction policy, new parent personas are created via **person-evidence**, not extraction. Patrick Flynn (I1) remains unlinked to this record's persona until person-evidence confirms the match and creates the parental link.
- **Relationships (spouse, parent-child)** are indirect, researcher-inferred — flagged accordingly.
- **Blank real-property value** for Thomas was correctly excluded — silence in a 1850 census is not reportable negative evidence.
- **Schuylkill County place resolution** flagged low-confidence (no state listed in source text itself).

---

### Recommended Next Steps

1. **`/person-evidence`** — link this record's Patrick persona to I1, and create/link Thomas and Mary Flynn as parent tree persons.
2. **`/check-warnings`** — resolve the Schuylkill County place and any other new warnings from this write.

**28 assertions written** across all four household members, all linked to `log_006` and `q_001`:

| Person | Tree ID | Assertions | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thomas Flynn | I2 (existing) | 7 | name, sex, ~b. 1818, Ireland, laborer, Schuylkill Co. residence, head-of-household |
| Bridget Flynn | **I5 (new stub)** | 7 | name, sex, ~b. 1843, Ireland, child-of-Thomas (inferred) |
| Patrick Flynn | I1 (existing) | 7 | name, sex, ~b. 1845, Ireland ⚠️, Schuylkill Co. residence, child-of-Thomas |
| John Flynn | **I6 (new stub)** | 7 | name, sex, ~b. 1848, Pennsylvania, child-of-Thomas (inferred) |

**Tree changes:** Two new parent–child edges added — Thomas (I2) → Bridget (I5) and Thomas (I2) → John (I6). The I1–I2 edge already existed.

**Key classification note:** Pre-1880 census doctrine applies — respondent identity is unrecorded, so name/age/birthplace/occupation are `information_quality: indeterminate` (not primary). Residence alone is primary (enumerator's direct observation). All parent–child relationships are `evidence: indirect` (inferred from household position, no wife listed).

**⚠️ Conflict flagged (assertion a_037):** Patrick's birthplace is recorded as **Ireland** in this census, but q_001 documents him as born in Pennsylvania. This is recorded faithfully with a bias note — not suppressed. The parentage assertion for Patrick (a_038) is marked tentative pending corroboration.

**No wife/mother recorded** in this household, so no maternal relationship was created.

---

**Recommended next step:** Seek an independent record — original death certificate, church baptism, or naturalization paper — to resolve the Ireland-vs.-Pennsylvania birthplace conflict before treating Thomas as a confirmed father for q_001.

Good — the project has one person already in the tree: **Patrick Flynn (I1)**. No open questions, no sources yet. I'll log the user-provided record first, then delegate to the extractor.Log written as `log_001`. Now delegating to the record-extractor agent.Here's what the extractor wrote:

---

## Extraction Complete — `src_001` / S1

**Source classification:** Derivative (original image not examined — extraction was from your pasted transcript, no census scan reviewed).

### 26 Assertions Across 4 Persons

| Person | Role | Assertions |
|---|---|---|
| Silas Kerrigan | Boarder | 6 |
| Thomas Flynn | Head of household | 7 |
| Mary Flynn | Wife | 6 |
| Patrick Flynn (→ I1) | Child | 7 |

Each person received assertions for: **name, sex, age, estimated birth year, birthplace, residence** (Schuylkill Co., PA, 1860), and **occupation** where listed.

### GPS Three-Layer Classifications Applied

- **Source layer:** Derivative (transcript, not original image)
- **Information layer:** Indeterminate — pre-1880 census respondent is unknown; ages and birthplaces carry this flag rather than primary/secondary
- **Evidence layer:** Direct for stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation); Indirect for computed birth years and all inferred relationships (no relationship column exists in 1860 census)

### Relationship Assertions
Spouse link (Thomas ↔ Mary) and parent-child links (Patrick ↔ Thomas, Patrick ↔ Mary) were written as **indirect/inferred** — the 1860 census has no relationship-to-head column, so co-residence + compatible ages + shared surname is the basis.

### Key Flags
- 🔴 **Thomas Flynn and Mary Flynn are not yet tree persons.** The parent-child edges to **I1 Patrick Flynn** cannot be finalized until they are established as persons — this is a job for **person-evidence** and/or **proof-conclusion**.
- 🔵 **Silas Kerrigan** is recorded as a differently-surnamed co-resident (boarder). No kinship asserted — he's a FAN-cluster lead worth investigating separately.
- ⚠️ **Original not examined** — the source is derivative. Locating and reviewing the actual 1860 census image would upgrade the source classification and may resolve the indeterminate information ratings.

### Recommended Next Step
Run **person-evidence** to create Thomas Flynn and Mary Flynn as tree persons and wire up the parent-child relationships to Patrick Flynn (I1).

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted 26 assertions with mostly correct factual values and appropriate evidence classifications. However, it failed to surface the FAN lead about Silas Kerrigan's potential kinship to the Flynn family as a hypothesis-tracking lead in the assertions themselves. The test context explicitly requires noting that Silas Kerrigan (b. ~1798) heading a household containing the Flynn family could indicate kinship (e.g., Mary's father), with 'Kerrigan' as a candidate maiden name. While the response text mentions this as a 'FAN-cluster lead,' the skill did not create any assertion or structured note capturing this investigative hypothesis for downstream tracking. The skill also correctly avoided fabricating a cross-surname relationship assertion, but the kinship possibility should have been more formally captured in the research state, not just mentioned in narrative.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed most of what was required: extracted assertions for all four household members, computed birth years from ages, identified occupations for Silas and Thomas (but correctly omitted them for Mary and Patrick per the test requirement), and flagged that Thomas and Mary are not yet tree persons. However, it incompletely addressed the FAN lead requirement. The test context states the skill 'must treat the differently-surnamed head as a FAN lead' and 'surface in the presentation as a lead for hypothesis-tracking.' While the response text mentions Silas as a 'FAN-cluster lead,' the skill did not create a formal assertion, note, or structured entry in the research state to capture this lead for ongoing investigation. The kinship hypothesis (Silas as potential father of Mary, Kerrigan as maiden name) was mentioned but not persisted in a way that downstream skills can query or track.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All five MCP tool calls passed arguments matching the fixture's expected behavior. The project_context calls correctly queried the project state; research_log_append correctly logged the user-provided record with appropriate metadata; research_append correctly appended 26 assertions and one source entry with proper structure and classifications; tree_edit's add_household_children call correctly attempted to link Patrick to Thomas and Mary (the skip due to 'no_parent_in_tree' is expected idempotent behavior, not a tool-argument error). No calls landed in fixture_not_found; all args were semantically correct.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birthplace, and occupation are separate `a_` entries with distinct `fact_type` values. Birth date (computed year) and birth place are both `birth` assertions but distinguished by populated fields (`date` vs `place`), which is correct per the rubric. Relationship assertions are atomic (spouse_inferred, child_inferred). No compound claims mixing multiple facts into one `value` field. The structure supports downstream querying of individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is correct across all assertions. For census name/age/birthplace/occupation facts, the skill correctly identified the informant as 'unknown household member' with proximity 'household_member,' distinguishing the recorder (census enumerator) from the actual reporter. For residence facts, the skill correctly assigned 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness,' which is the correct doctrine for census residence (the enumerator personally observed the dwelling). The `information_quality: indeterminate` flag appropriately reflects pre-1940 census convention where the respondent is unknown. Relationship assertions correctly use `informant: none — inferred from household position` with proximity `researcher`.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation, residence) are marked `direct` — the census explicitly states these in its columns. Computed birth years are marked `indirect`, correctly reflecting inference from stated age. Relationship assertions (spouse_inferred, child_inferred) are marked `indirect` because the 1860 census has no relationship column; the relationships are inferred from household position, co-residence, shared surname, and compatible ages. The skill correctly avoided marking census-stated facts as indirect based on 'the household member didn't witness the birth' — that would invert the doctrine. All classifications are defensible under mainstream GPS doctrine.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: This dimension is a duplicate of the earlier 'Informant identification' rubric dimension. Scoring it again: informant identification is correct. The skill properly distinguished the census enumerator (recorder/witness for residence) from the unknown household member (informant for name/age/birthplace/occupation). Relationship inferences correctly use `researcher` as the informant with proximity `researcher`. No informant is blank when context exists to identify it. The skill correctly applied the census-enumerator/witness doctrine for residence facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The extraction is mostly correct but contains a critical informant-proximity error on residence assertions. The skill correctly marks residence as direct evidence and correctly identifies the census enumerator as the informant. However, it assigns informant_proximity 'witness' to the enumerator for residence facts, which is correct per the per-test context and census doctrine. The actual error is on non-residence facts: for name, age, birthplace, occupation, and relationship assertions, the skill uses informant_proximity 'household_member' (correct) but the informant field says 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' or similar. This is acceptable per the rubric's 'unknown household member' guidance. However, the per-test context explicitly states that for the 1900 census with an explicit relationship column, relationship assertions should be marked as direct evidence with household_member proximity—which the skill does correctly. The main issue is that the skill does not create separate relationship assertions for each child to each parent (child->head is direct, child->spouse-of-head is indirect per the per-test context), yet the per-test context explicitly permits and even encourages this split. The skill only creates one relationship assertion per child (to the head), missing the opportunity for the more precise child->spouse-of-head indirect assertion. This is an incompleteness issue rather than a correctness error in what was persisted, but the persisted state is correct for what was done.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed most of what was required but missed one significant piece: the per-test context explicitly states that 'The skill splitting a child's relationship one-per-parent and marking child->head DIRECT and child->spouse-of-head INDIRECT is CORRECT and more precise.' The skill created only one relationship assertion per child (to the head, George), but did not create a second relationship assertion for each child to Sarah (the spouse). For Charles and Emma, the skill should have created separate assertions: 'son of George Bennett' (direct, stated in column) and 'son of Sarah Bennett' (indirect, inferred from household structure since the column only states relationship to head). The skill extracted 34 assertions but should have extracted 36 (two additional child->spouse relationship assertions). All other required facts (names, ages, birthplaces, occupations, school attendance, residence, couple relationship) were extracted and correctly classified.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments matching the fixture's expected behavior. The skill made six tool calls: project_context (twice), research_log_append, research_append (twice), tree_edit (twice). All calls succeeded with ok: true and matched.kind: 'live'. The research_append call correctly passed 34 assertion entries with proper structure, and the tree_edit calls correctly added four persons and five relationships. The geocoding correction (updating standard_place for Springfield assertions from the incorrect Jamaica location to the correct Illinois location) was handled via a second research_append call with update operations, which is correct recovery behavior. No fixture_not_found errors occurred.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. The skill correctly decomposed compound information: name, sex, race, age, birthplace (place), birth year (date), occupation, residence, relationship, and education are all separate assertions with their own fact_type. The skill correctly kept date and place as attributes of a single birth event (not splitting them into separate assertions), which is the correct interpretation of atomicity. No compound assertions mixing multiple facts into one value field were found.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is mostly correct but has one systematic issue. For census facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation), the skill correctly identifies the informant as 'unknown household member' with proximity 'household_member', which is appropriate per the rubric's guidance that a household member reported these facts. For residence, the skill correctly identifies 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness', which is the correct doctrine for census residence (the enumerator personally observed the dwelling). For relationships, the skill correctly identifies 'unknown household member' with proximity 'household_member'. However, the skill does not create the second relationship assertion (child->spouse) that would require informant_proximity 'researcher' (inferred from household structure, not stated in the column). The missing assertions prevent full evaluation of this dimension, but what was persisted is defensible.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence type classification is mostly correct but incomplete. The skill correctly marks all stated facts as direct: name, sex, race, age, birthplace (place), occupation, residence, and relationship assertions are all marked 'direct', which is correct for a post-1880 census with explicit relationship column. The skill correctly marks computed birth years as 'indirect'. However, the skill does not create the child->spouse relationship assertions that would be marked 'indirect' (inferred from household structure, not stated in the column). The per-test context explicitly states this split is correct and more precise. For the assertions that were created, evidence_type is accurate and defensible. The incompleteness (missing child->spouse assertions) prevents a full pass, but the classifications on persisted assertions are correct.

output.text_response:
Good. The project is active with one existing person (Patrick Flynn, I1) and no sources yet. I'll log the user-provided census record, then delegate to the extractor.Logged as `log_001`. Now delegating to the record-extractor agent.Here is the extraction summary:

---

**Source created:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1900 U.S. Federal Census, Springfield, Sangamon County, Illinois (user-provided)

**Assertions: 34 total**

| Person | Tree ID | Assertions |
|---|---|---|
| George Bennett (Head) | I2 | 8 — name, sex, race, age, birth year (~1855), birthplace (Ohio), occupation (farmer), residence |
| Sarah Bennett (Wife) | I3 | 8 — name, sex, race, age, birth year (~1858), birthplace (Ohio), relationship, residence |
| Charles Bennett (Son) | I4 | 9 — name, sex, race, age, birth year (~1884), birthplace (Illinois), occupation (farm laborer), relationship, residence |
| Emma Bennett (Daughter) | I5 | 9 — name, sex, race, age, birth year (~1888), birthplace (Illinois), school attendance, relationship, residence |

**Relationships added:** Couple R1 (George–Sarah), ParentChild R2–R5 (George and Sarah → Charles; George and Sarah → Emma)

**GPS Three-Layer Classifications Applied:**
- **Source layer:** Original (census schedule — the enumerator's contemporaneous record)
- **Information layer:** Indeterminate for self-reported facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation — informant unidentified per pre-1940 census doctrine); Primary for relationship and residence (household member with firsthand knowledge)
- **Evidence layer:** Direct for all stated facts; Indirect for calculated birth years

**One geocoding issue caught and corrected:** The place resolver initially mapped "Springfield, Sangamon County, Illinois" to Springfield, Jamaica — the extractor overrode this and set the correct U.S. location on all four residence assertions (a_008, a_016, a_025, a_033).

**No link to Patrick Flynn (I1)** — the Bennett household stands as an independent family unit.

**Recommended next step:** Run **check-warnings** on persons I2–I5, particularly to verify the residence `standard_place` values that were manually corrected after the geocoding miss.

| Person | Assertions |
|---|---|

| **Bridget Dolan** (I6, new stub) | Name (mother of bride) |
| **Rev. Daniel O'Connor** | Name (officiant) |

**Tree changes:** 5 new person stubs (I2–I6), 4 parent-child relationships (R1–R4), 1 couple relationship (R5) with marriage fact F1 — 15 Oct 1870, Church of the Annunciation, Shenandoah, PA.

---

**Key classification notes:**
- **Birthplace assertions** → *secondary* information (neither party can witness their own birth)
- **Age, residence, occupation, parents' names** → *primary* information (self-reported at time of marriage)
- Thomas Flynn and James Gallagher noted as dual-role (parents *and* witnesses)

Good. Now I'll delegate to the record-extractor agent.Here's what was extracted and persisted from the obituary:

---

**Source created:** `src_001` (S1) — Harold Dean Whitaker obituary, Logan, Utah, 2021
**Total assertions:** 26 across all named persons

**Tree persons created:**
| ID | Name | Role |
|----|------|------|
| I2 | Harold Dean Whitaker | Subject |
| I3 | Walter Whitaker | Father |
| I4 | Edith Whitaker | Mother |
| I5 | Ruth Ann Baxter | Wife |
| I6 | Robert Whitaker | Son |
| I7 | Linda Whitaker | Robert's wife (stub) |
| I8 | Linda Merrill | Daughter |
| I9 | Paul Merrill | Linda's husband (stub) |
| I10 | Grace Tolman | Sister (+alt name Grace Whitaker) |

**Relationships recorded:** Walter–Edith couple (R1); parentage to Harold (R2/R3); Harold–Ruth marriage 3 Jun 1952, Logan LDS Temple (R4); Harold→Robert and Linda Merrill as children (R5/R6); Robert–Linda couple (R7); Linda Merrill–Paul couple (R8).

---

**GPS classification applied to all assertions:**
- **Source:** Authored work (obituary, derivative)
- **Information:** Indeterminate — obituary author unnamed; some facts (birth date/place) are secondhand by nature
- **Evidence:** Direct — each fact is explicitly stated

---

**Flags to note:**
- **Place geocoder drift:** Logan, Brigham City, and Twin Falls initially resolved to European namesakes — corrected manually. Worth verifying the standard_place values.
- **Inferred surnames:** Robert's wife "Linda Whitaker" and "Paul Merrill" — surnames are conventional inferences, not explicitly stated; flagged in bias notes.
- **Grace's parentage:** Not re-stated in the obituary, so no Walter/Edith → Grace parent-child edges were added; her sibling relationship to Harold is captured via assertion only.
- **7 grandchildren / Karen Foster:** Recorded as assertions; no unnamed person stubs created.

**Recommended next steps:** Run `check-warnings` on the new tree persons, then `person-evidence` to review Harold (I2) and verify place resolutions.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All extracted facts are factually accurate and supported by the obituary text. Names, dates, places, relationships, and biographical details match the source. The skill correctly identified the two Lindas as distinct persons (Linda Whitaker as daughter-in-law, Linda Merrill as daughter), correctly parsed Grace (Whitaker) Tolman as a maiden-name convention, and correctly identified Karen Foster as a neighbor rather than kin. No fabricated material or unsupported claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all major facts from the obituary: Harold's birth, death, marriage, parents, children (both biological and spouses), sister, and neighbor. All named persons were captured with their relationships and residences. The skill addressed everything the user message and source material required. No silent omissions of items the obituary made obvious.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments matching the expected behavior. The skill correctly called project_context, research_log_append, research_append (with 26 assertions), tree_edit (add_person and add_relationship operations), and corrected place geocoding errors in a follow-up update call. No fixture_not_found errors. The place-correction recovery (initial geocoding to European locations, then corrected to US locations) was a single clean recovery with correct args on retry.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Compound information is properly decomposed: name, age, residence, death, birth (with separate date and place), marriage, and relationships are all separate assertions. No assertion mixes multiple distinct facts into one value field. The skill correctly created separate assertions for birth date and birth place, and for marriage date and place.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill consistently identified the informant as 'unknown obituary author (likely family member or funeral home staff)' with informant_proximity 'unknown' across all assertions. While the proximity could have been more specific in some cases (e.g., family members reporting on their own household), the approach is defensible for an obituary where the author is not named. The skill correctly identified Karen Foster as a neighbor (not kin) and did not misattribute informants.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly assigned as 'direct' for facts explicitly stated in the obituary. The skill correctly recognized that stated names, ages, residences, dates, and relationships in the obituary are direct evidence. No facts were incorrectly marked as indirect. The classification is consistent with the doctrine that stated facts in a source are direct evidence.

output.text_response:
I'll kick both off at the same time — the record-extraction skill for the indexed record, and the image-reader agent for the page scan. They'll run in parallel.No narration guidance found — defaulting to one-line preambles. Logging the user-provided record before delegating.Logged as `log_001`. Delegating to the record-extractor agent now.Here's what was extracted and persisted:

---

**Source S1 / src_001** — *Brazil, Pernambuco, Civil Registration, 1804-2023* (ark:/61903/1:1:QLWC-Y1P7)
Classified **derivative** because the extraction was from FamilySearch's indexed fields only — the original register page scan (ARK 3:1:3QS7-99QG-KBTG) returned a 1×1 pixel stub and could not be read. The "original register image not examined" flag is attached to the source.

---

**14 assertions extracted (a_001–a_014):**

| Person | Assertions |
|---|---|
| **I2 Severino Ramos da Silva** (groom) | Name, age (26), residence (Recife), birthplace (Recife), marriage |
| **I3 Rivaneta Florencio de Araujo** (bride) | Name, age (21), residence (Recife), birthplace (Caruaru), marriage |
| **I4 João Ramos da Silva** | Name (groom's father) |
| **I5 Maria da Conceição** | Name (groom's mother) |
| **I6 Miguel Florencio** | Name (bride's father) |
| **I7 Josefa de Araujo** | Name (bride's mother) |

**Relationships:** Couple R1 (I2–I3) with marriage fact 22 Aug 1957, Recife; parent–child pairs R2/R3 (groom ← I4, I5) and R4/R5 (bride ← I6, I7).

---

**GPS classifications applied:**
- Marriage date/place → *primary source / primary information / direct evidence* (civil registrar acting in official capacity)
- Self-reported personal facts (name, age, residence) → *primary / primary / direct*
- Birthplaces → *primary / **secondary** / direct* — nobody witnesses their own birth, so birthplace testimony is secondary information even from the party themselves

---

**On the image scan:** The ARK 3:1:3QS7-99QG-KBTG is currently unreachable (1×1 placeholder). Once FamilySearch makes it available, pulling that scan would let you verify the indexed spelling of all names and upgrade the source classification from derivative to original. Worth bookmarking.

---

**Next steps:** Run `/person-evidence` to review the six new persons, and re-attempt the image scan when the ARK becomes live.

---

**Source created:** `src_001` — *United States Census, 1850* (FamilySearch, original)

**7 assertions extracted:**

| ID | Person | Assertion |
|---|---|---|
| a_001 | Patrick Flynn | Name: Patrick Flynn |
| a_002 | Patrick Flynn | Birthplace: Ireland |
| a_003 | Patrick Flynn | Birth year (calculated): ~1845 |

- **Source layer:** Original (the 1850 census enumeration itself)
- **Information layer:** `indeterminate` for name/age/birthplace — pre-1880 census, respondent unknown; a household member could have firsthand knowledge of the child's birthplace, so "secondary" doesn't automatically apply
- **Evidence layer:** Relationship (Thomas → Patrick) classified as **indirect / researcher-inferred** — pre-1880 census household proximity, no explicit relationship column

**Tree changes:**
- Patrick Flynn (I1) now cites `src_001`

**Recommended next steps:**

Since search-records apparently ran without a `projectPath`, there's no existing log entry — I'll create one, then delegate to the record-extractor agent.Good — log entry `log_001` written. ---

### Extraction — Source S1 (`src_001`)
**Record:** 1850 U.S. Census, Patrick Flynn in household of Thomas Flynn (FamilySearch, original)

**7 assertions written:**

| Assertion | Classification |
|---|---|
| Name: Patrick Flynn (subject) | Original source · Primary information · Direct evidence |
| Name: Thomas Flynn (household head) | Original source · Primary information · Direct evidence |
| Gender: Male | Original source · Primary information · Direct evidence |
| Birth place: Ireland | Original source · Primary information · Direct evidence |
| Birth date: ~1845 (calc. from age) | Original source · Primary information · Indirect evidence |
| Residence 1850: Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA | Original source · Primary information · Direct evidence |
| Relationship: child of Thomas Flynn (inferred) | Original source · Secondary information · Indirect evidence |

**Tree changes:** Patrick Flynn (I1) linked to S1. No Thomas Flynn stub created — 1850 census carries no explicit relationship column, so the parent entry is held for person-evidence/proof work rather than asserted at extraction.

---

### ⚠️ Flag — Geocoding issue
`Branch Township, Schuylkill, Pennsylvania` resolved to **Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada** by the place tool — a known ambiguity when no country is supplied. This needs a manual correction via `tree_correct` before the residence assertion is used in any geo-based analysis.

---

### FamilySearch Matches on MXHY-TP4
Two collateral matches returned:

| Record | Confidence |
|---|---|
| **1860 U.S. Census** (pid MABC) | 4 / high |
| **Pennsylvania Death Certificates 1906–1967** (pid MDEF) | 3 / moderate |

Both are pending — not yet extracted. The **1860 census (confidence 4)** is the strongest next lead: it would extend the Thomas Flynn household link by a decade and help confirm this is the same Patrick Flynn. Want me to pull and extract that one next?

output.text_response:
Good. The index shows:
- **Child:** Christian Frederich, born 11 Jul 1817, christened 20 Jul 1817, Stavanger, Rogaland
- **Father (indexed):** Tollev Nadnesen, residence: Birkeland
- **Mother (indexed):** Inger Larsdatter

"Tollev Nadnesen" is a suspect transcription — "Nadnesen" is not a recognizable Norwegian patronymic, the child's surname "Tollefsen" implies father's given name was *Tollef* (not matching "Tollev Nadnesen"), and the source note flags the uncertainty. Per the skill protocol, the indexed value alone cannot stand as established fact — I need to pull the original register image. Let me load volume_search.`volume_search` is not available in this environment. I'll log the `record_read`, then route immediately to the image-reader agent to check the original Stavanger church book page — the source note on the record flags an image group reference which I can use as the entry point.Good — log_001 written. Now routing to the image-reader to verify the father's name directly from the register scan.The image is over the transport-safety limit — NOT READ. Per the skill protocol, I will not treat the indexed value as confirmed, and will not retry the same image. I now delegate to the record-extractor with a **tentative flag** on the father's surname, as the original cannot be examined.Here's the full picture from this extraction:

---

## Extraction complete — ark:/61903/1:1:68Q3-5SGC

**Source:** src_001 / S1 — *Norway, Church Books, 1797–1958* (classified `derivative` because the original image could not be read this session)

**7 assertions written:**

| Assertion | Person | Value | Quality / Status |
|-----------|--------|-------|-----------------|
| a_001 | Child | Name: Christian Frederich | Direct / Primary |
| a_002 | Child | Birth: 11 Jul 1817, Stavanger | Direct / Primary |
| a_003 | Child | Christening: 20 Jul 1817, Stavanger | Direct / Primary |
| a_004 | **Father** | Name: **"Tollev Nadnesen [?]"** | Secondary / **Tentative** |
| a_005 | Father | Residence: Birkeland, 1817 | Direct / Primary |
| a_006 | Mother | Name: Inger Larsdatter | Direct / Primary |
| a_007 | Mother | Residence: Stavanger, 1817 | Direct / Primary |

---

## Why the father's surname is marked `[?]`

Your suspicion was right to flag it. The index transcribes the father as **"Tollev Nadnesen"**, but:

- **"Nadnesen" is not a recognizable Norwegian patronymic.** Standard -sen formations derive from a father's given name (e.g., "Tollefsen" = son of Tollef). "Nadne-" has no obvious root.
- **The child's own surname "Tollefsen"** corroborates that the father's given name was Tollef or a close variant — the indexed "Tollev" is consistent with that. Only the *surname* is in doubt.
- **The register image was unreachable** (transport-safety limit exceeded), so the manuscript spelling cannot be confirmed this session.
- **Most likely correction: "Aadnesen"** (son of Aadne/Aadni) — the leading *Aa-* is a common OCR/transcription slip to *Na-* in Gothic script. Other possibilities: a different patronymic, or a farm name wrongly captured in the surname column.

**What *is* established:** The father's given name was **Tollev/Tollef**, his residence was **Birkeland** (which matches the child's farm surname), and the mother was **Inger Larsdatter**.

---

## Outstanding step to resolve the father's identity

Examine the original Stavanger parish register image directly. The image group reference associated with this record exceeded the inline transport limit — try accessing it via the FamilySearch image viewer at:

> `https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:68Q3-5SGC` → "View image"

Look specifically at the *father's name column* for the 20 July 1817 entry: if the first letter of the surname is a tall script *A* (often written *Aa-*), the reading is almost certainly **Aadnesen**, not Nadnesen.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified the suspect transcription 'Nadnesen' as anomalous, extracted all verifiable facts (child's name, birth/christening dates and places, mother's name and residence, father's residence), and marked the father's surname as tentative with [?] and secondary information_quality. The skill did not finalize the suspect required identifier as confident. All persisted assertions match the record_read response and are factually accurate. The source classification as 'derivative' is correct given the image was not examined. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed the user's core request: extract the father from the baptism record. It extracted all available facts from the record (child's name, birth, christening, mother's name and residence, father's given name, residence, and surname with appropriate uncertainty flagging). The outstanding step (original-image confirmation) was explicitly named. No required assertions were omitted; blank fields produced no assertions per protocol.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments matching expected values. record_read was called with the correct recordId (ark:/61903/1:1:68Q3-5SGC, matching ~68Q3-5SGC). research_log_append and research_append calls used correct projectPath and structured arguments. No tool calls returned fixture_not_found errors. Arguments were semantically correct throughout.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. The child's birth is one assertion with both date and place fields (correct per schema design, not compound). The father's name is separate from residence. The mother's name is separate from residence. No assertions mix distinct facts into a single value field. All seven assertions are properly atomic.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are specifically identified: 'presenting parent (unspecified)' for child's name/birth, 'officiant' for christening, 'presenting parent (likely the father himself)' for father's name/residence, 'presenting parent (likely the mother herself)' for mother's name/residence. Informant_proximity values are appropriate: household_member for parent-reported facts, official_duty for the officiant's christening record. The skill correctly distinguished the informant from the recorder and applied household_member proximity where a parent reported household facts.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence_type values are correct. The child's name, birth, and christening are marked direct (stated in the record). The father's name is marked direct (stated in the index, even though the surname spelling is suspect—per the grading note, transcription doubt is an information_quality/accuracy matter, not an evidence_type downgrade). Father's and mother's residences are direct (stated). The skill correctly applied the stated-vs-inferred doctrine and did not conflate accuracy-doubt with inference.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants and their proximity. For the suspect father's surname, the informant is marked 'presenting parent (likely the father himself)' with proximity 'household_member'—appropriate for a household member reporting on himself. The informant_bias_notes field explicitly flags the transcription doubt and explains why the surname is marked [?] and secondary. The skill did not treat the indexed value as a confident informant report; instead, it marked the information_quality as secondary and flagged the outstanding image-verification step.
